title: Joshua Wong Foreign Collusion Hearing — Case Proceeding at High Court
summary: High Court hearing held March 6, 2026 for foreign collusion charge carrying life imprisonment — next hearing date pending
details:
Joshua Wong's foreign collusion hearing took place on March 6, 2026 at Hong Kong's High Court. This is a SEPARATE case from his HK47 subversion conviction (4 years 8 months, appeal dismissed Feb 2026).

THE CHARGE:
• Conspiring with Nathan Law (now in UK exile) and others to encourage foreign countries to impose sanctions on Hong Kong/China
• Period: July–November 2020
• Maximum sentence: LIFE IMPRISONMENT
• Case was transferred to the High Court — indicating prosecution is seeking a severe sentence

WHY THIS MATTERS:
• If convicted, Wong could face life in prison on top of his existing 4yr 8mo sentence
• Amnesty International calls the charges 'designed to prolong his stay behind bars' and prevent his scheduled January 2027 release
• The case represents an escalation in the use of the National Security Law against political opposition

ACTION:
• Share Joshua Wong's case on social media
• Contact your representatives to raise his case
• Monitor hearing outcomes via Hong Kong Watch and Amnesty International

title: URGENT: Jimmy Lai Sentenced to 20 Years in Prison
summary: Hong Kong media tycoon Jimmy Lai sentenced to 20 years under NSL — confirmed he will not appeal
details:
On December 15, 2025, Jimmy Lai was found guilty on all charges including "conspiracy to collude with foreign forces" under Hong Kong's National Security Law. On February 9, 2026, he was sentenced to 20 years in prison — the harshest sentence yet under the NSL.

As of March 2026, Lai has confirmed he will NOT appeal the conviction (NBC News). The UN has specifically called for his release. Lai, age 78, faces the remainder of his life behind bars.

ACTION NEEDED:
• Contact your representatives to demand his release
• Share his story on social media to raise awareness
• Support the Committee for Freedom in Hong Kong
• Sign petitions calling for sanctions on HK officials

title: 100+ Global Brands Linked to Uyghur Forced Labor
summary: Major investigation identifies over 100 brands in forced labor supply chains; UFLPA entity list now at 144 companies
details:
A joint investigation by The New York Times, The Bureau of Investigative Journalism, and Der Spiegel (May 2025) found over 100 global brands tied to Uyghur forced labor via a Chinese state-imposed labor transfer system. Separately, the US UFLPA Entity List has grown to 144 banned companies (37 added January 2025).

KEY FINDINGS:
• Fashion, technology, automotive, food sectors — Apple, Volkswagen, BMW, Tesla, KFC
• Critical minerals (titanium, lithium, beryllium) supply chains deeply linked
• Estimated 13.75 million labor transfers planned in Xinjiang's current five-year plan
• EU forced labor regulation coming into effect 2027

WHAT YOU CAN DO:
• Check the UFLPA Entity List before making purchases
• Contact companies to demand supply chain transparency
• Support the Coalition to End Forced Labour in the Uyghur Region

title: Hong Kong 47: Appeals Dismissed — Sentences Upheld
summary: Court of Appeal upholds convictions; Amnesty International condemns ruling
details:
The Hong Kong 47 case — the largest prosecution under the National Security Law — saw all 45 activists sentenced to 4-10 years in prison. On February 23, 2026, the Court of Appeal dismissed appeals from 12 defendants, upholding all convictions and sentences.

NOTABLE SENTENCES:
• Benny Tai: 10 years
• Joshua Wong: 4 years 8 months (appeal dismissed Feb 2026)
• Claudia Mo: 4 years 2 months
• Gwyneth Ho: 7 years

Amnesty International called the rejected appeals 'a missed opportunity to start restoring justice.' The ruling confirms Hong Kong's judiciary provides no meaningful check on politically motivated NSL prosecutions.

title: ESCALATION: Family Member Prosecuted Under Article 23
summary: Father of US-based activist sentenced for handling daughter's finances — first family prosecution
details:
On February 26, 2026, Kwok Yin-sang was sentenced to 8 months in prison in Hong Kong for handling the financial assets of his daughter Anna Kwok, a US-based pro-democracy activist and executive director of the Hong Kong Democracy Council.

This marks the FIRST known case of a family member being prosecuted under Article 23 national security provisions for providing financial support to a designated 'absconder.'

WHY THIS MATTERS:
• Sets dangerous precedent — family members are now targets
• Extends transnational repression to relatives who remain in Hong Kong
• Designed to isolate overseas activists by threatening their families
• Could deter Hong Kongers from supporting exiled relatives

ACTION:
• Share this case to raise awareness of Article 23's expanding reach
• Support organizations documenting transnational repression
• Contact representatives about sanctions targeting officials involved
